claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 14 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
circumstances: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin. To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your situation.

**First, let's talk about your child:**

How old is the child you're claiming Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know the age of your child (or children, if you're claiming for more than one).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm actually asking about two of my kids - I've got Alex who's 14, and then Blake who's 19.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you check the eligibility for both Alex (14) and Blake (19). Since they're in different age categories, we'll need to go through some different checks for each.

Let's start with **Alex (age 14)**:

Since Alex is under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit. However, there's one more thing I need to check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question for Alex (age 14):**

Is Alex working **less than 24 hours per week** (or not in paid work at all)?

This is usually straightforward for younger children, but I need to confirm. Does Alex work fewer than 24 hours per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - they're only 14, still at school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's what I expected for a 14-year-old! Let me continue with Alex's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question for Alex (age 14):**

Is Alex **NOT receiving** any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

(This would be unusual for a 14-year-old, but I need to confirm.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't get any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question for Alex (age 14):**

Is Alex currently in an apprenticeship?

Given that Alex is 14 and still at school, I'd expect the answer is no, but I just need to confirm they're not in any kind of apprenticeship programme.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't doing an apprenticeship - they're just at regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Alex is progressing well through the checks. Now I need to ask about **your** situation as the claimant.

**Question about your residency:**

Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?

1. **Normally live in the UK**
2. **Have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Have pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. **Have pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work
5. **Have pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself and family
6. **Have pre-settled status** and are a student with sufficient resources
7. **Have pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Living abroad** as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

You only need to meet **one** of these. Which best describes your situation? (For most people, option 1 - "normally live in the UK" - applies.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, living in the UK meets the residency requirement. 

**Question about your responsibility for Alex:**

Are you responsible for Alex? This can be met in any of the following ways:

1. **You live with Alex**
2. **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** (e.g., paying for food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc. - at least equal to the Child Benefit amount)
3. **You pay maintenance** for Alex under a court order or agreement

Does any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me - they live with my ex-partner.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Even though Alex doesn't live with you, you can still be eligible if you contribute to Alex's upkeep.

**Follow-up question:**

Do you contribute financially to Alex's care? This could include:
- Paying for food, clothes, or pocket money
- Contributing to housing costs
- Giving presents or other support
- Paying maintenance under a court order or agreement

The contribution needs to be at least equal to the Child Benefit amount (currently around £25.60 per week for the eldest child, or £16.95 for additional children).

Do you contribute to Alex's upkeep in any of these ways?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not currently contributing financially to Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand. Unfortunately, without either living with Alex or contributing to their upkeep, you wouldn't meet the responsibility requirement for claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I've noted that you're **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex because you don't currently live with them or contribute to their upkeep.

---

Now let's check your eligibility for **Blake (age 19)**. Since Blake is between 16 and 19, there are different rules that apply.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question for Blake (age 19):**

Since Blake is between 16 and 19, they need to be in approved education or training to qualify. Which of the following best describes Blake's current situation?

1. **Currently in education or training** (e.g., A-levels, T-levels, NVQ, college course, etc.)
2. **Recently left education or training** (within the last few months)
3. **Neither** - not in education/training and hasn't been recently

Which applies to Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake is in education - they're studying for their A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
A-levels are on the approved qualifications list. I just need to confirm a few more details about Blake's education:

**Questions about Blake's A-level studies:**

1. **Is Blake studying full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study/tuition)

2. **Is the course funded by an employer** or part of an arrangement where Blake has already agreed to a job with them afterwards?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake is studying full-time - it's a normal college course. And no, it's not funded by an employer or anything like that - just regular A-levels at sixth form.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great - A-levels at sixth form is exactly the kind of approved education that qualifies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question for Blake (age 19):**

Is Blake working **less than 24 hours per week** in paid employment (or not working at all)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't work - they're just focused on their studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question for Blake (age 19):**

Is Blake **NOT receiving** any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't get any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question for Blake (age 19):**

Just to confirm - Blake is studying A-levels, so they're **not** in an apprenticeship programme, correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, Blake isn't doing an apprenticeship - just A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Good! You've already told me you live in the UK (in Manchester), so you meet the residency requirement. Let me move on to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about your responsibility for Blake:**

Now I need to ask about Blake. Are you responsible for Blake? This can be met in any of the following ways:

1. **You live with Blake**
2. **You contribute to Blake's upkeep** (e.g., paying for food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc. - at least equal to the Child Benefit amount)
3. **You pay maintenance** for Blake under a court order or agreement

Does any of these apply to you for Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That meets the responsibility requirement for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Question about other claimants for Blake:**

Is anyone else currently claiming (or planning to claim) Child Benefit for Blake?

- **No** - no one else is claiming for Blake
- **Yes, but we've agreed** - someone else was claiming, but they've agreed to transfer it to you
- **Yes, and there's a dispute** - multiple people want to claim and there's no agreement

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, yes actually - my ex-partner already claims Child Benefit for Blake, and Blake lives with them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Ah, I see! Thank you for clarifying. This is important information - you mentioned earlier that Blake lives at home with you, but now you've said Blake lives with your ex-partner who already claims Child Benefit for them.

Just to make sure I understand correctly: **Where does Blake actually live?**

1. **With you** in Manchester
2. **With your ex-partner**
3. **Splits time between both households**

This will help me determine the correct eligibility outcome for Blake.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Sorry, I should have been clearer - Blake does live with me here in Manchester. But my ex-partner is the one who's already claiming the Child Benefit for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
No problem, thank you for clarifying! So Blake lives with you, but your ex-partner is currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake.

**Important question:** Have you and your ex-partner agreed about who should be claiming Child Benefit for Blake? For example:

1. **You've agreed** that your ex-partner will stop claiming and transfer the claim to you
2. **There's a dispute** - you both want to claim and haven't reached an agreement
3. **You're happy** with your ex-partner continuing to claim

Which best describes the situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: There's no agreement - we both want to claim it and haven't sorted it out.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand - this is a situation where HMRC will need to step in to make a decision.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for all the information. I now have enough to give you the assessment outcome for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Summary of Your Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment

### Alex (age 14) - ❌ **NOT ELIGIBLE**

Although Alex meets the age and other basic requirements, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex because:
- Alex doesn't live with you (they live with your ex-partner)
- You're not currently contributing financially to Alex's upkeep

**To become eligible for Alex**, you would need to either have Alex live with you, or start contributing at least the Child Benefit amount (approximately £25.60/week for a first child) towards their care.

---

### Blake (age 19) - ⏸️ **DEFERRED TO HMRC**

You meet all the eligibility requirements for Blake:
- ✅ Blake is in approved education (A-levels, full-time)
- ✅ Blake doesn't work 24+ hours per week
- ✅ Blake doesn't receive benefits in their own name
- ✅ You live in the UK
- ✅ Blake lives with you

**However**, because your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake and you both want to claim, HMRC will need to decide who receives the benefit.

**What HMRC will consider:**
1. Who Blake lives with most of the time (this appears to be you)
2. Who contributes most to Blake's upkeep
3. Blake's best interests
4. Continuity of existing arrangements

**Next steps for Blake:**
- Contact the Child Benefit Office to submit your claim
- Provide evidence that Blake lives with you (e.g., school records, GP registration, council tax records)
- HMRC will review both claims and make a binding decision

📞 **Child Benefit Office:** 0300 200 3100  
🔗 **More information:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit-child-lives-with-someone-else

---

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about your situation?

reasonings: Alex is 14 years old (under 16), which meets the age requirement. Alex doesn't work, doesn't receive benefits in their own name, and isn't in an apprenticeship. You meet the UK residency requirement (living in Manchester). However, you are NOT eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex because you don't meet the 'responsibility' requirement - you don't live with Alex (they live with your ex-partner) and you're not currently contributing financially to Alex's upkeep.

Blake is 19 years old and is studying A-levels full-time at sixth form, which is approved non-advanced education. Blake doesn't work 24+ hours per week, doesn't receive benefits in their own name, and isn't in an apprenticeship. You meet the UK residency requirement and the responsibility requirement (Blake lives with you). However, there is a dispute because your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake and you haven't agreed who should claim. This means HMRC will need to make the final decision - the outcome is DEFERRED to HMRC.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex because you don't live with them or contribute to their upkeep. For Blake, although you appear to meet the eligibility criteria (Blake lives with you and is in approved education), there is a dispute with your ex-partner who is already claiming. HMRC will need to decide who should receive the benefit for Blake.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Payments cover the child's upkeep
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.guidance: You must be responsible for the child, which means either living with them OR paying at least the Child Benefit amount towards their care (food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.). This can include maintenance payments under court order.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.reason: Multiple people claim responsibility for the child and cannot agree who should claim Child Benefit
response.guidance: When two or more people are responsible for a child and cannot agree who should claim, HMRC will make the decision. HMRC considers: (1) Who the child lives with most of the time (primary residence), (2) Who contributes most to the child's upkeep, (3) The child's best interests, (4) Continuity of existing arrangements. Both parties should contact the Child Benefit Office with evidence of their circumstances. HMRC's decision is binding. Common scenarios: separated parents with shared custody, child living part-time with each parent, grandparent vs parent both contributing.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 14 years old (under 16), which meets the age requirement. Alex doesn't work, doesn't receive benefits in their own name, and isn't in an apprenticeship. You meet the UK residency requirement (living in Manchester). However, you are NOT eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex because you don't meet the 'responsibility' requirement - you don't live with Alex (they live with your ex-partner) and you're not currently contributing financially to Alex's upkeep.

Blake is 19 years old and is studying A-levels full-time at sixth form, which is approved non-advanced education. Blake doesn't work 24+ hours per week, doesn't receive benefits in their own name, and isn't in an apprenticeship. You meet the UK residency requirement and the responsibility requirement (Blake lives with you). However, there is a dispute because your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake and you haven't agreed who should claim. This means HMRC will need to make the final decision - the outcome is DEFERRED to HMRC.

response.overall_reasoning: You are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex because you don't live with them or contribute to their upkeep. For Blake, although you appear to meet the eligibility criteria (Blake lives with you and is in approved education), there is a dispute with your ex-partner who is already claiming. HMRC will need to decide who should receive the benefit for Blake.
